A memorable and melancholy history is related in this chapter and the next, about Israel turning back from the borders of Canaan and being sentenced to wander and perish in the wilderness because of their lack of belief and complaining. It appears that the idea to search out the land came from the people. They had a better opinion of their own plans than of God's wisdom. We ruin ourselves by believing reports and appearances rather than Divine revelation. We walk by sight, not by faith. Moses gave the spies this instruction: Be courageous. It was not only a great undertaking that they were assigned, which required good management and determination; but also a great trust was placed in them, which required faithfulness. Courage in such circumstances can only come from strong faith, which Caleb and Joshua alone possessed.

The searchers of the land brought a bunch of grapes with them, along with other fruits, as proof of the goodness of the country; which to Israel was both an assurance and a sample of all the fruits of Canaan. Such are the present comforts we have in communion with God, foretastes of the fullness of joy that we expect in heavenly Canaan. We can see by them what heaven is.

We may wonder that the people of Israel waited forty days for their spies to return, when they were ready to enter Canaan, under all the assurances of success they could have from Divine power and the miracles that had previously accompanied them. But they distrusted God's power and promise. How much we hinder ourselves by our lack of belief! Eventually, the messengers returned; but most people discouraged others from going forward to Canaan. Justly are the Israelites left in this temptation for putting confidence in the judgment of men when they had the word of God to trust in. Though they found the land as good as God had said, they would not believe it was as sure as he had said, but despaired of having it, even though Eternal Truth had guaranteed it to them. This was the representation of the evil spies. Caleb, however, encouraged them to go forward, supported only by Joshua. He does not say, Let us go up and conquer it; but, Let us go and possess it. Difficulties that are in the way of salvation diminish and disappear before a lively, active faith in God’s power and promise. All things are possible if they are promised to him who believes; but carnal sense and carnal professors are not to be trusted. Unbelief overlooks the promises and power of God, magnifies every danger and difficulty, and fills the heart with discouragement. May the Lord help us to believe! We will then find all things possible.
